coredata.dc:description: COVID-19 pandemic has significantly influenced the dietary habits of humans, emphasizing the incorporation of natural ingredients to enhance immunity towards viral and bacterial infections. Curcumin (Cur), a widely used traditional medicine in various Asian countries and a natural coloring agent, has gained popularity, leading to surge in its usage specially in post COVID-19 era. This surge has led to increased scrutiny of the potential side effects of excessive Cur use, with recent reports suggesting it may result in inactivation of DNA and reduce adenosine triphosphate levels, leading to health risks. In this work, we synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 72.9 % for the sensitive and selective detection of Cur. The developed fluorescent probe exhibits excellent sensory response towards Cur within a concentration range of 0.081–51.45 µM, achieving an ultra-low detection limit of 15.91 nM. The sensor was successfully tested on real food samples like ginger powder, turmeric powder, and curry powder, demonstrating good recovery rates. To assess the practicality of the sensor system, we developed a 3D-printed smartphone-integrated device platform for curcumin detection through fluorescence image analysis. This developed platform exhibited promising results, achieving a limit of detection (LoD) of 132.28 nM across a curcumin concentration range of 0.13–54.00 µM. This device platform holds significant potential for the development of efficient sensors for real-time detection of Cur in food samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization and surface functional groups of NCDs. (a) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image showing distribution of NCDs (yellow encircled), with size histogram of TEM image in the inset, (b) HR-TEM image of a single NCD with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, (c) FE-SEM image showing NCDs distributed all over the scan area and histogram size profile with an average size of 11.97 ± 1.65 nm in the inset, (d) FT-IR spectra of NCDs showing different functional groups.

XPS characterization of GQDs. (a) Full XPS survey spectrum of NCDs revealing the presence of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen along with calculated elemental composition mentioned in the image, (b) the high resolution deconvoluted spectra of C 1s along with that of (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s.

Optical characterization of GQDs. (a) absorption and PL spectrum of NCDs, (b) excitation dependent PL emission of NCDs under excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm, (c) PL response of NCDs in water with a pH varying from 4 to 11. Inset- PL spectra of NCDs at different pH, (d) effect of NaCl concentration on PL emission of NCDs indicating good stability in ionic solutions. Inset: PL Spectra of NCDs at different NaCl concentrations. (e) PL emission of NCDs under continuous excitation at 365 nm for 2 h and (f) PL response of NCDs taken over time, from the day of synthesis till 140 days of storage showcasing excellent photostability. Inset: PL spectra of NCDs over storage days.

Sensory performance and investigation of sensing mechanism. (a) Fluorescence response of NCDs towards Cur across a concentration range from 0.08 µM to 51.45 µM with inset image showing NCD solution and NCD-Cur solution, both under UV lamp, (b) Ksv plot showing the linear response of NCDs with respect to Cur concentration, (c) spectral overlap between absorption spectrum of Cur and fluorescence spectrum of NCDs, (d) zeta potential of Cur and NCD, (e) fluorescence lifetime study of NCDs before and after the addition of Cur (f) HOMO-LUMO energy levels of NCDs determined from UPS spectra and Tauc plot, compared with that of Cur.

Selectivity of the sensor system. (a) Selectivity of NCDs towards various biomolecules and (b) ions in comparison with Cur, (c) fluorescence response of NCDs towards Cur in the presence of interfering analytes, (d) Cell viability study (MTT assay) towards different concentrations of NCDs.

3D-printed smartphone integrated device platform. (a) Design model of the device platform showing various components, (b) assembled view of the device platform, (c) real image of the disassembled 3D printed device platform and (d) assembled device platform attached to a smartphone while image capture, (e) series of fluorescence images captured by smartphone corresponding to varied concentrations of curcumin, (f) linear relationship of quenching efficiency versus concentration of curcumin and (g) smartphone application showing the mode of operation where first window uploads the control and sample image and second window displays the RGB values along with the concentration of curcumin in the sample.
(a) Synthesis scheme of bright fluorescent NCDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine. (b) Schematic illustration of the curcumin detection using NCDs.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots as analytical probe for sensitive detection of curcumin through smartphone integrated 3D-printed platform: A new horizon in food safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: COVID-19 pandemic has significantly influenced the dietary habits of humans, emphasizing the incorporation of natural ingredients to enhance immunity towards viral and bacterial infections. Curcumin (Cur), a widely used traditional medicine in various Asian countries and a natural coloring agent, has gained popularity, leading to surge in its usage specially in post COVID-19 era. This surge has led to increased scrutiny of the potential side effects of excessive Cur use, with recent reports suggesting it may result in inactivation of DNA and reduce adenosine triphosphate levels, leading to health risks. In this work, we synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots with a photoluminescence quantum yield of 72.9 % for the sensitive and selective detection of Cur. The developed fluorescent probe exhibits excellent sensory response towards Cur within a concentration range of 0.081–51.45 µM, achieving an ultra-low detection limit of 15.91 nM. The sensor was successfully tested on real food samples like ginger powder, turmeric powder, and curry powder, demonstrating good recovery rates. To assess the practicality of the sensor system, we developed a 3D-printed smartphone-integrated device platform for curcumin detection through fluorescence image analysis. This developed platform exhibited promising results, achieving a limit of detection (LoD) of 132.28 nM across a curcumin concentration range of 0.13–54.00 µM. This device platform holds significant potential for the development of efficient sensors for real-time detection of Cur in food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data generated and analyzed for the study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur) is a polyphenolic compound derived from Curcuma longa, extensively used as a spice in traditional medicine and cuisine, particularly in Asian countries [1]. The medicinal benefits of Cur have been extensively investigated, revealing its pharmacological and physiological effects, such as anti-inflammatory [2], anti-cancer [3,4], antibacterial [5] and antiviral responses [6]. Due to these advantages, physicians recommend it for treating various ailments like inflammation, depression, allergies, and cancer [7,8]. Moreover, Cur is rapidly replacing synthetic agents in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, dietary foods, and food coloring [9–11]. The broad acceptance of Cur for these applications originates from the assumption that it poses minimal adverse health effects compared to its benefits [12–14]. Some studies have reported that excessive use of Cur can lead to deactivation of DNA and disrupt the adenosine triphosphate (ATP) levels in cells, potentially leading to health risks [15,16]. In addition, reports suggest that elevated doses of Cur could result in gastrointestinal issues and allergic reactions [17]. Investigations concerning Cur’s interactions with other medications are limited, with experts suggesting possible adverse effects such as blood clotting, diarrhea, etc. if used without proper medical advice [18,19]. The World Health Organization recommends a daily intake of Cur ranging from 0 to 3 mg/kg body weight, especially considering its widespread use in the food industry [20]. These concerns have garnered increased attention following the COVID-19 pandemic, prompting individuals to seek naturally occurring agents to bolster immunity and gain potential medical benefits [21]. Cur is among the select few such agents anticipated to witness widespread use by both individuals and industries, particularly as an additive in food matrices [22]. A study conducted in Jordan on dietary habits aimed at boosting immunity during COVID-19 pandemic reported a nearly 16 % increase in the use of curcumin, in the form of turmeric [22]. In addition, clinical trials evaluating the effectiveness of curcuminoids on hospitalized COVID-19 patients have shown promising results, further encouraging the public to incorporate curcumin into their diets. [23]. Consequently, it is crucial to develop and implement facile strategies for the sensitive determination of Cur, especially in food matrices.

A range of analytical techniques has been developed for the sensitive detection of Cur in food samples. These methods primarily include electrochemistry [24–27], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [28], electrophoresis [29], spectrometry [30] and liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [31]. Although these methods offer accuracy, selectivity, and sensitivity, they all require skilled analysts, sophisticated equipment, complex sample preparation protocols, and prolonged analysis times. To address these limitations, an alternative approach is designing chemical sensors, establishing an analytical platform that utilizes optical signals for detection [32–37]. Recent efforts have focused on developing efficient sensors, particularly fluorescence sensors [38–41], that enhance selectivity and sensitivity, also offering rapid analysis and simplicity. Key advantages of fluorescence-based sensing strategy are simplicity, speed, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness [42]. Given Cur’s weak fluorescence, various fluorescent probes, especially nanoprobes, have been developed for the direct or indirect detection of Cur. The fluorescent nanoprobes reported for Cur detection include metal nanoclusters [43], silicon nanoparticles [44], polymer nanoparticles [45], upconversion nanoparticles [46], silver-doped graphene carbon nitride nanosheets [47], etc. Nevertheless, these probes frequently face challenges such as weak biocompatibility, poor photostability and cytotoxicity.

These challenges can be addressed by leveraging carbon dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial that demonstrates excellent photophysical properties. CDs also possess desirable properties such as excellent photostability, high biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and superior water stability than conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [48]. Due to their remarkable optical features, CDs have shown significant promise in multiple applications, including chemical and biological sensing, drug delivery, bioimaging and photodynamic therapy [49]. Among the strategies to enhance the luminescence properties of CDs, nitrogen doping stands out as highly effective [50]. Introducing defects through nitrogen incorporation results in an upward shift in the Fermi level, which improves the electron mobility in the conduction band. This leads to a substantial enhancement of the photophysical properties of the CDs [51]. While numerous studies have explored the utilization of carbon dots derived from various carbon sources for Cur detection [51–57], achieving a high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) in CDs is crucial for enhancing the sensory response.

In this study, we employed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as a fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Cur. The NCDs were synthesized via a one-step pyrolysis method using citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, achieving an excellent PLQY of 72.9 %. The synthesized NCDs exhibited outstanding photostability, low cytotoxicity, and a robust sensory response to Cur. Our investigations revealed that Cur effectively quenched the fluorescence of the synthesized NCDs through the inner filter effect, with a detection limit of 15.91 nM over a concentration range from 0.081 µM to 51.45 µM. Moreover, the developed fluorescent nano sensor system was successfully tested in food samples such as ginger powder, turmeric powder, and curry powder, yielding satisfactory results and excellent recovery rates. The practical application of the sensor system was evaluated by developing a 3D-printed device platform integrating smartphone-based fluorescence image analysis for curcumin detection. The device platform employs an ultra-violet (UV) surface mounting device light emitting diode (SMD LED) as the excitation source, quartz cuvette for sampling, along with an in-house developed smartphone application for curcumin detection though image analysis. The developed device platform could detect curcumin along a concentration range starting from 0.13 µM to 54.00 µM with a detection limit of 132.28 nM. This cost-effective device platform, with high sensitivity and ease of use, holds promise for onsite detection of Cur in food samples, effectively addressing concerns surrounding food safety.

Citric acid (CA) was acquired from Merck, India and Ethylenediamine (EDA) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Curcumin (Cur) and all other analytes (analytical grade), including biomolecules, amino acids, cations, and anions, used for selectivity studies, were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) was used for all experiments, dilutions, and sample preparations. Details regarding the instruments used for the experiments are provided in the ESI.

NCDs were prepared through one–step thermal pyrolysis of CA with ethylenediamine, following a previously reported bottom-up approach with minor modifications [58]. First, 3.5 g CA was dissolved along with 1.12 ml of EDA in 10 mL ultrapure water. The beaker containing the solution was kept at 100 °C in a hot air oven for 24 h. An orange powder was obtained, which was then dispersed in DI water and purified by passing it through a 0.2 µm syringe filter (13 mm id, Axivia Sichem Pvt. Ltd., India). The solution was subsequently dialyzed using a 3 kDa dialysis membrane for 48 h and then lyophilized to collect the powder (Scheme 1 a).

UV–Visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a quartz cell with 1 cm path length. 4 mg of NCD powder was dispersed in 4 ml ultrapure water to prepare the stock solution. Approximately 300 µL of the NCD stock solution was introduced into the cell, followed by the addition of varying concentrations of Cur, which was separately dissolved in methanol. A 2.5 ml volume of PBS buffer solution (pH 7) served as the primary solvent. Cur was dissolved in methanol since methanol is miscible with water. The mixture was thoroughly homogenized at room temperature, after which it was subjected to fluorescence measurements, with the excitation wavelength set at 350 nm and PL emission recorded at 449 nm (Scheme 1b). The sensory response towards NCDs was tested against Cur and other potential analytes such as bovine serum albumin (Alb), bilirubin (Bil), creatinine (Cre), glucose (Glu), L-cysteine (L-Cy), L-Glutathione (L-Gl), L-tryptophan (L-Trpt), L-tyrosine (L-Try), Urea (Ure), L-Alanine (L-Al), Ascorbic acid (Asc), Aspartic acid (Asp), Glycine (Gly), Leucine (Leu), Maltose (Mal), Phenylalanine (PheAn) and Sucrose (Suc). In addition to these interfering analytes, a wide range of ions including Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Pb2+, Hg+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cl-, Br−, I-, SO4 2−, CH3COO−, NO3 2− and NO3 3− were also tested for fluorescence response towards NCDs.

L-132 human embryonic lung epithelial cell lines were obtained from the National Center for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. The cells were preserved in DMEM (High glucose) media with 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA) and 1 % antibiotic/antimycotic mix kept at 37 degrees Celsius and 5 % CO2, in humid conditions. For washing, cell culture grade 1× phosphate buffer saline (PBS) from Himedia, Mumbai, India, was used. Trypsin-EDTA from Gibco, USA, was utilized for sub-culturing cells. Firstly, L-132 cells were seeded in a tissue culture grade 96 well plate with a density of about 5 × 103 cells per well. The cell lines were incubated for 24 h after which the cells were exposed to increasing concentrations of NCDs and further incubated at 37 °C for the desired amount of time. After the required incubation time, 10 µL of MTT solution from stock concentration of 5 mg mL−1 was introduced to each well (per 100 µL media) under dark conditions. This was followed by a 4-hour incubation period under the same environmental conditions after which, the medium was removed, and the formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 % DMSO. Absorbance of the samples were then recorded at 570 nm to calculate the cell viability. The experiment was performed on three repeats. The cell viability was calculated as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis by NCDs based sensor system
ce:section.ce:para: Ginger powder, turmeric powder and curry powder were obtained from a local market in Roorkee, India. The real samples were pretreated following methods reported in the literature, with slight modifications [59]. Specifically, 0.2 g of each sample was dissolved in 5 mL methanol and stirred rigorously for 10 min. The solutions were then centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 20 mins, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.45 µm, 13 mm id cellulose syringe filter (Axivia Sichem Pvt. Ltd., India) to remove larger particles. The filtered samples were further diluted with methanol to fall within the detection concentration range. A portion of the pretreated samples was then directly analyzed for Cur concentration using HPLC, while the remaining samples were spiked with Cur. The samples were spiked with 5 µM and 10 µM Cur and further analyzed using the NCD-based fluorescence detection strategy where PL spectra was recorded, as detailed in the previous section. The concentration of the curcumin in the samples were determined by the linear relationship derived from Ksv plot as explained in Section 3.4. The concentration in spiked samples were also evaluated using the HPLC technique to compare the performance of the developed sensor system with conventional HPLC detection method.

The concentration of curcumin in food samples was analyzed using a Waters 2998 series HPLC system (Waters, USA) equipped with a variable wavelength detector (VWD). The chromatograms were recorded by monitoring the VWD at a wavelength of 420 nm. The separation was achieved using a 250 mm × 4.6 mm LC-18 reverse phase stainless steel column (Supelco, Bellefonte, PA, USA) and was employed in conjunction with a C18 guard column (ReliantTM C18, 5 μm, Part no. 186007283, Waters, Ireland). The mobile phase consisted of a 75: 25 (v/v) mixture of acetonitrile (ACN) and 5 % glacial acetic acid (HAc). The flow rate was maintained at 1.0 mL/min, with an injection volume of 20 μL, and the column temperature was controlled at 30 ± 1 °C.

To evaluate the feasibility of the NCD-based sensor system for curcumin detection, a compact 3D-printed device platform was developed and tested. The CAD model was created using AUTOCAD FUSION 360 software and subsequently fabricated using a Fusion Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printer (Raise3D E2, USA). The platform consists of three primary components: a cuvette holder designed to accommodate a 3.5 ml cuvette along with a closing lid, a smartphone holder, and a camera focus tube which can be attached to it. For the excitation of NCDs, a UV SMD LED (Vishay VLMU1610-365-135, USA) with a peak excitation wavelength of 367 nm was selected and integrated into the LED slot of the device platform. An optical filter (400–1000 nm) was positioned between the smartphone holder and camera focusing tube to block excitation from the SMD LED.

Additionally, an in-house RGB color analyzer android application was developed using Python platform to convert captured color images into RGB color space, providing the respective RGB values. The operation of the device platform begins by inserting the quartz cuvette containing 200 µL of NCDs (from the stock solution) in 2.5 mL PBS Buffer (pH 7.0). Once the lid is secured and the camera focusing tube is attached to the cuvette holder platform, a controlled dark environment is established within the platform, enabling fluorescence imaging. The smartphone application was first calibrated by introducing varying concentrations of Cur (0.13–54 µM) into the NCD solution followed by analyzing the RGB values obtained from the images. Quantitative detection of Cur was achieved by considering the B-channel values among the RGB data and plotting the values against Cur concentration, thereby arriving at a calibration equation. During calibration, each sample was analyzed three times to ensure accurate detection. The entire device platform was spray-coated with matte black spray paint to prevent any optical interference during image capture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characterization of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structure, particle size and morphology of the synthesized NCDs were characterized using XRD, HR-TEM and FE-SEM. The XRD pattern of NCDs shows a broad peak centered around 23.90, indicating a graphite-like structure consistent with previous reports (Fig. S1a) [60]. The broad nature of XRD peak highlights the amorphous carbon phase of the NCDs. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) reveals that the synthesized NCDs are uniformly distributed, with the size histogram (inset in Fig. 1a) showing particles ranging from 7 to 13 nm, with an average size of 10.03 ± 1.41 nm, as determined using Image J software. Fig. 1b presents a high-resolution TEM image of a single NCD, showing a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the 002 plane of the graphite, corroborating the structural findings from XRD analysis. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S1b) further confirms the amorphous nature of the NCDs. To gain further insights into the size distribution of NCDs, FE-SEM analysis was performed, providing a detailed microscopic view of NCDs at a magnification of 66120x. FE-SEM image (Fig. 1c) shows nanosized particles, with the size histogram (inset) indicating an average size of 11.97 ± 1.65 nm. Therefore, HR-TEM and FE-SEM characterizations confirm the formation of NCDs with average sizes ranging from 10 to 12 nm.

To investigate the surface functional groups, present in the NCDs, FT-IR spectroscopy was conducted, revealing multiple functional groups on the surface of NCDs (Fig. 1d). The FT-IR spectra display a broad, intense peak centered around 3360 cm−1, corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl group and further the hydrophilic nature of the NCDs. Additionally, N–H stretching vibration at 3218 cm−1 and C–H stretching vibration at 2942 cm−1 were observed [61]. The peaks at 1690 cm−1 and 1546 cm−1 indicate the presence of C=O and C=C groups, respectively [58,61]. Furthermore, C–N stretching vibration at 1334 cm−1 and C–H bending vibration at 1397 cm−1 were also identified [61,62]. Absorption bands located at 1650 cm−1 and 1170 cm−1 are associated with C=N stretching vibration and C–O bending vibrations, along with C–OH stretching vibration at 1066 cm−1 [51,61]. These results confirm the presence of multiple functional groups attached to the NCDs, as well as successful nitrogen doping. To further support the FT-IR characterizations and examine the chemical composition, XPS characterization was carried out on the NCDs. The full survey XPS spectrum of the synthesized NCDs shows three predominant peaks at binding energies around 284.6 eV, 531.6 eV and 399.9 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution C 1s spectra was deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 2b), corresponding to C=C/C–C, C=O, C–O and C–N with respective peak positions at 284.6 eV, 285.4 eV, 287.5 eV and 288.3 eV [51,61,62]. The O 1s spectrum comprises of three peaks (Fig. 2c) at 530.6 eV, 532.1 eV and 532.7 eV, which are assigned to –OH, C=O and C–O, bonds, respectively [61,63]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum consists of three peaks at 397.7 eV, 398.8 eV and 400.2 eV (Fig. 2d), corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen, amino nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen [61,63,64]. The elemental composition of NCDs, calculated from the XPS spectra, revealed 62.6 % of oxygen, 31.2 % of carbon and 6.2 % of nitrogen, respectively. Therefore, both FT-IR and XPS analyses confirm successful nitrogen doping into the carbon dot structure, with multiple oxygen containing functional groups attached to it.

The optical characteristics of NCDs were explored using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum shows an absorbance peak at 345 nm, corresponding to n–π* transition of C=O bond, while the fluorescence spectra reveal a maximum emission centered at 449 nm (Fig. 3 a). The NCDs displayed excitation dependent PL emission, due to its heterogenous size distribution, as evident from HR-TEM and FE-SEM analyses (Fig. 3b). The CIE chromaticity coordinates (0.16, 0.13) of the NCDs were obtained by plotting the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. S2), which was derived from the fluorescence spectrum. The PLQY of the blue-emitting NCDs was determined to be 72.9 % in water at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, using quinine sulfate (PLQY = 54.4 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference standard (Fig. S3), with detailed calculations provided in the ESI.

Photostability is a critical parameter for fluorescent nanoprobes, which can be significantly influenced by numerous factors like pH, ionic concentration, illumination time, storage periods and dilutions. For an optimal fluorescent probe, it is essential that its fluorescence properties remain stable under diverse environmental conditions. Therefore, photostability of the NCDs were examined by testing their PL response across different pH values, ionic concentrations, continuous UV radiation exposure, long storage periods and dilutions. As depicted in Fig. 3c, NCDs demonstrated a reduction in the PL intensity under strong acidic and alkaline conditions but maintained excellent PL emission in solutions having pH values in the range of 6.0–9.0. This behavior correlated with FT-IR analysis, where the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface of NCDs were revealed, can get disrupted in strong acidic and alkaline conditions leading to quenching of PL emission [65]. These observations highlight that NCDs are excellent fluorescent probes suitable for applications in neutral environments. The NCDs were also tested in ionic solutions with varying concentrations of NaCl and the resulting PL responses were recorded (Fig. 3d). It can be deduced from the recorded spectra that NCDs remain unaffected by changes in ionic concentrations, making them ideal probes for fluorescence studies in high ionic-strength solutions. Furthermore, NCDs were subjected to continuous UV irradiation (365 nm excitation) using a 5 W Xenon lamp for 2 h. As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the NCDs exhibited minimal photobleaching even after prolonged exposure to UV radiation. The photostability of NCDs were also evaluated over a storage period of more than 140 days. Fig. 3f presents the PL spectra of NCDs recorded at equal intervals over this period, showing only an 8 % reduction in the PL intensity even after storing it for 140 days, compared to PL intensity generated on the day of synthesis, indicating its chemical stability over months. The dilution stability of the NCDs was also evaluated by preparing a series of dilutions from the stock solution at 10x, 30x, 50x, 100x, 500x and 1000x dilutions. First, the photostability of each NCD dilution was tested by continuously exciting the solutions for 10 min, with measurement taken at one minute interval. The NCDs demonstrated remarkable photostability at each dilution, with minimal reduction in PL intensity, as shown in Fig. S4a–g. Apart from these observations, the PL intensity of each dilution was recorded, and the ratio of the PL intensity of the stock solution to that of each dilution was plotted against the dilution factor. Fig. S4h depicts the highly linear relationship of the PL intensity with respect to the dilution factor (R2 = 0.998), ensuring consistent performance of NCDs across varying concentrations and prolonged use. Collectively, these studies underscore the potential of NCDs as robust fluorescent probes for investigations in systems under the influence of complex environments.

The sensory response of the NCDs towards Cur is influenced by several factors, with pH and reaction time being particularly critical for detection sensitivity. To examine the effect of pH on the fluorescence response of NCDs towards Cur, PL spectra of NCDs were recorded after adding a fixed concentration of Cur (4.2 µM) to solutions with varying pH ranging from 4 to 11. As shown in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of NCDs varies with pH, where Io and I represent the PL intensity of NCDs before and after addition of Cur, respectively. The quenching efficiency increases up to pH 7 and then decreases, with pH 7 providing the optimal response for Cur detection. Similarly, reaction time was also studied by monitoring the PL spectra of NCDs over time after the addition of Cur. It is observed that the quenching response of NCDs towards Cur is rapid, reaching saturation within 1 min, as depicted in Fig. S5b. Based on these observations, the optimum pH and reaction time was determined to be pH 7 and 1 min for all further investigations.

Following the comprehensive characterizations of the NCDs and confirmation of their excellent photostability under various critical conditions, we investigated the fluorescence response of NCDs towards Cur and other potential analytes. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots have been extensively studied as fluorescent probes for detecting numerous metal ions and other analytes over the years [66]. Hence, we started with investigating the sensory response of synthesized NCDs towards Cur as well as evaluating their selectivity by introducing multiple interfering analytes into the sensor system. The sensory response of NCDs was initially investigated by recording the PL spectra of NCDs across a range of Cur concentrations, from 0.081 µM to 51.45 µM. It can be observed in Fig. 4 a that Cur effectively quenched the fluorescence of NCDs linearly. This highly sensitive response of NCDs towards Cur was quantified by constructing Stern-Volmer plot, where the concentration of Cur was plotted along the x axis and (Io/I) − 1 on the y axis (Fig. 4b). Here, Io and I represent the fluorescence intensity of NCDs at 449 nm before and after addition of Cur concentrations. The respective relation is defined as:
(1)
I o
I
= 1 +
Q
K
s v
where [Q] represents the concentration of Cur and Ksv denotes Stern-Volmer constant, which quantifies the sensitivity of the system. The Ksv plot as shown in Fig. 4b, exhibits a linear correlation described by fit equation Io/I − 1 = 0.337 [Q] + 4.206 × 10−5, with a high correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.996 and Ksv constant of 3.37 × 105 M−1. The inset image in Fig. 4a visually demonstrates the fluorescence quenching of NCD before and after the addition of Cur (NCD-Cur) under UV lamp. The limit of detection (LoD) for the sensor system was determined based on calibration plot, which correlates the fluorescence intensity of NCDs at each Cur concentration with respect to concentration of Cur, yielding an LoD of 15.91 nM. Fig. S6a shows the Ksv plot for the NCD response across the entire range of Cur concentrations, while Fig. S6b illustrates the calibration plot, with further calculations provided in the ESI. Additionally, the sensory response of NCDs towards various analytes such as biomolecules, amino acids, cations, anions, etc. has been investigated and is comprehensively detailed in the selectivity study section. Since, Cur was dissolved in methanol for the PL sensing experiments, which involved adding aliquots of Cur to the NCD solution in ultrapure water, it is essential to assess the photostability of NCDs in methanol. Fig. S7a illustrates the PL spectra after the addition of various amounts of methanol, demonstrating excellent photostability of NCDs. The maximum amount of methanol added during fluorescence sensing experiments was 600 µL, which resulted in a minute reduction of 0.8 % signal intensity. Additionally, the fluorescence response of NCDs after the addition of methanol (600 µL) was recorded under continuous UV irradiation for 100 min, as shown in Fig. S7b. The NCDs exhibited remarkable stability in methanol, excluding the possibility of any interaction between NCDs and methanol, proving the sensing assay to be optimal for Cur detection.

Detailed investigations were conducted to explore the underlying mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching of NCDs in the presence of Cur. Fig. 4c illustrates the spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cur and PL spectrum of NCDs. The significant overlap between the two spectra suggests three possible mechanisms: Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET), Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and Inner Filter Effect (IFE). PET involves an excited-state electron transfer process wherein the excited electron from the fluorescence probe is transferred into the analyte, resulting in fluorescence quenching [67]. FRET occurs through non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling between NCDs and Cur [68]. Both FRET and PET require the fluorescent probe and analyte to be in proximity (less than 10 nm). IFE doesn’t involve any interaction between fluorescence probe and analyte. Instead, the emission from the fluorescence probe is directly absorbed by the analyte, thereby reducing the PL signals detected [69]. This phenomenon is commonly referred to as secondary IFE wherein a substantial spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of analyte and PL spectra of fluorescence probe is a prerequisite. To investigate the potential interaction between NCDs and Cur, zeta potential study was conducted to analyze the surface charge of both NCDs and Cur. The zeta potential values of NCDs and Cur were determined to be −31.3 mV and −16.3 mV, respectively (Fig. 4d). The zeta potential measurements were conducted in ultrapure water, where NCDs were already dispersed in ultrapure water and Cur dissolved in methanol-ultra pure water mixture before being introduced into ultrapure water solution. The highly negative zeta potentials of both NCDs and Cur suggest that electrostatic interactions between them are highly unlikely, effectively excluding the possibility of such interactions. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs was measured using TCSPC both with and without the addition of Cur. The fluorescence decay of NCDs was accurately fitted to a bi-exponential decay function, as shown in Fig. 4e yielding an average lifetime of 13.57 ns. The lifetime of NCDs exhibited negligible change after the addition of 50 μM concentration of Cur, resulting in a lifetime of 13.45 ns (Table S1, ESI). The detailed explanation of the bi-exponential fit equation and the calculation of fluorescence lifetime is provided in detail in the ESI.

A negligible change in fluorescence lifetime of NCDs after the addition of Cur, coupled with the highly negative zeta potentials of both NCDs and Cur, strongly suggests that FRET is not the mechanism behind the fluorescence quenching of NCDs upon the addition of Cur. Another potential mechanism is the formation of a ground state complex between NCDs and Cur, which was investigated through UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy of NCDs at varying concentrations of Cur. As shown in Fig. S8, no shift in the absorbance peak corresponding to NCDs was observed, apart from an increase in the absorbance of Cur peak at ∼425 nm with the addition of Cur. Lastly, to ascertain the likelihood of PET within the system and to comprehend the energy level structure of NCDs, UPS studies were conducted to determine the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) levels of NCDs. HOMO and LUMO energy levels are determined from the relations as described below [70]

energy levels of NCDs were calculated to be −6.13 eV and −2.93 eV, respectively based on relations Eq. (2), Eq. (3) and Eq. (4). The HOMO-LUMO energy level calculations are explained in detail in the ESI. Additionally, the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of Cur were reported to be −2.22 eV and −5.75 eV, respectively [71]. In accordance with these calculations, Fig. 4f illustrates the HOMO-LUMO energy levels of both NCDs and Cur, revealing that the excited electrons in the LUMO of NCDs cannot transition or transfer into the LUMO of Cur, as the LUMO of Cur is at higher energy level. These observations collectively confirm IFE as the sensing mechanism responsible for the sensory response of NCDs to Cur, which can be attributed to the spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of Cur and PL spectra of NCDs. The photons emitted by the NCDs are directly absorbed by the Cur before it reaches photodetector, thereby reducing the PL signals, which in turn is reflected in the form of fluorescence quenching of NCDs – a phenomenon known as secondary inner filter effect. Table 1 presents the comparison between various reported strategies for curcumin detection with the developed sensor system. The results show that the developed sensor system is comparable with existing strategies and offers several advantages, such as high sensitivity, simple operation, and the use of low-cost reagents. Another key advantage of the developed sensor system is the rapid response time, which is significantly shorter compared to other reported methods. The performance of the developed sensor system was further evaluated by comparing its detection parameters with existing reports on Cur detection using fluorescent probes (Table S2). The developed NCDs based sensor demonstrated superior sensitivity compared to most of the reported works, paving the way for more efficient and innovative strategies for Cur detection.

The selectivity of the sensor system towards potential interfering analytes were evaluated based on the previous reports on analyte sensing utilizing NCDs. Thus, we investigated the sensory response of NCDs towards analytes such as album (Alb), bilirubin (Bil), creatinine (Cre), glucose (Glu), L-cysteine (L-Cy), L-Glutathione (L-Gl), L-tryptophan (L-Trpt), L-tyrosine (L-Try), Urea (Ure), L-Alanine (L-Al), Ascorbic acid (Asc), Aspartic acid (Asp), Glycine (Gly), Leucine (Leu), Maltose (Mal), Phenylalanine (PheAn) and Sucrose (Suc) as shown in Fig. 5 a. Apart from these analytes, some of the common food additives like citric acid (CitA), malachite green (MalG), monosodium glutamate (MSG), sodium benzoate (SodB), sunset yellow (SunY) and Tartrazine (Tar) were also tested for selectivity towards NCDs as depicted in Fig. 5a. The PL response of these food additives towards NCDs is shown in Fig. S10.

The concentration of Cur was fixed to be 4.2 µM and the concentration of all other analytes were fixed to be 10 µM for the investigations. Similar investigation was conducted to study the response of NCDs to a broad spectrum of cations and anions such as Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Pb2+, Hg+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cl-, Br−, I-, SO4 2−, CH3COO−, NO3 2− and NO3 3− alongside Cur (Fig. 5b). Here also, the concentration of Cur was maintained at 4.2 µM, while that of all other ions were fixed to be 10 µM. It can be observed from Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b that NCDs exhibit high selectivity towards Cur compared to diverse array of biomolecules, ions, and food additives. Fig. 5c illustrates the interference study where the PL response of NCDs towards Cur were also conducted in the presence of all other analytes tested for selectivity. Additionally, the interaction between the NCDs and analytes was further examined by conducting UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCDs after the addition of analytes. Figs. S11 and S12 shows the absorption spectra of NCDs compared to those of NCDs with added analytes (concentration of 10 µM). The results indicate that there is no shift in the absorbance peak of NCDs or the formation new peaks after analyte addition, except for the overlapping of absorbance peak of NCDs and respective absorbance peaks of analytes in the case of tartrazine and Fe3+. All these studies underscore remarkable selectivity of NCDs towards Cur in the presence of interferent biomolecules and ions.

To evaluate the cytotoxic effects of the synthesized NCDs, MTT assay was performed on L-132 cells. Initially, the L-132 cells were treated with increasing concentrations of NCDs (five concentrations starting from 20 µg/mL to 100 µg/mL). The cell viability for each concentration of NCDs was then determined and represented in Fig. 5d. The results clearly indicate that within the considered concentration range, NCDs exhibit minimal cytotoxic effects on L-132 cell lines, as no significant cell death was observed. This underscores the excellent biocompatibility of NCDs and further applicability for fluorescence sensing.

Monitoring of Cur concentration in food matrices is of utmost importance, given the recent increase in its utilization as a food additive. To validate the functionality of the developed sensor in real samples, we tested the sensor system on ginger powder, curry powder and turmeric powder. These pretreated samples were spiked with different concentrations of Cur (5 µM and 10 µM) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The amount of Cur was determined using the calibration curve obtained from Ksv plot as explained in Section 3.4. The HPLC technique was considered as the standard technique to compare our results. The initial concentration of Cur in the samples before spiking was determined through HPLC. As illustrated in Table 2 , the determined concentrations of Cur in the samples closely matched the spiked concentrations, with good recovery rates ranging from 92 %–103 %, as well as comparable with the results obtained from standard HPLC technique. The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) calculations are explained in the ESI. These results, along with other characterizations, demonstrate that the as-synthesized NCDs can be effectively applied for the detection and monitoring of Cur concentration in food samples, showing good reliability and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone based detection of Cur through 3D printed platform
ce:section.ce:para: To extend the applicability of the sensor system towards Cur detection, a portable and compact detection platform was developed using 3D printing, integrating with a smartphone application for analysis. The CAD model, shown in Fig. 6 a, shows a smartphone holder paired with the smartphone camera focusing tube along with an optical filter positioned between them. The cuvette holder includes two slots: one for attaching SMD LED and the other for the focusing tube (Fig. S13). These slots are strategically positioned opposite each other within the device platform to optimize the image capture efficiency via smartphone. Fig. 6b shows the fully assembled 3D model of the device platform, designed to replicate a dark room environment for fluorescence imaging. The 3D-printed platform, comprising the cuvette holder housing the SMD LED, the smartphone holder with the focusing tube, and the closing lid, is depicted in Fig. 6c. The SMD LED is powered by a 3.3 V supply from an Arduino mega board. Fig. 6d also shows the device platform under operation, with a smartphone capturing fluorescence images, with the cuvette containing the NCD solution kept inside it.

To calibrate the fluorescence imaging-based device platform, curcumin samples of varying concentration (0.13–54 µM) were first analyzed using the smartphone application. Fig. 6e displays an array of images captured by the smartphone, corresponding to samples with different curcumin concentration, along with a control sample (only NCDs). Fig. 6f showcases the linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency and curcumin concentration, after RGB analysis through smartphone application, where So represents blue-channel value of control and S represents that of the samples. The quenching efficiency exhibited excellent linear relationship with curcumin concentration across the entire range (0.13 µM to 54 µM), with an R2 value of 0.98 and a fit equation y = 0.01834x + 0.0425. Based on the calibration plot (Fig. S14), the LoD for the curcumin detection platform was calculated and found to be 132.28 nM. The LoD calculation is detailed in the ESI. After calibrating the smartphone application with the parameters, the operational interface of the application is as shown in Fig. 6g. The application comprises of two windows: the first facilitates the upload of control and the sample images, followed by the second window displaying the RGB values along with the concentration of curcumin in the sample. The obtained results underline the exceptional feasibility and applicability of the developed device platform for curcumin detection, using NCDs as the fluorescent probe. Table 3 highlights the performance comparison of the developed 3D-printed Cur detection platform with other reported smartphone-based Cur detection strategies. The developed platform offers several advantages over other reported strategies such as high sensitivity and compactness of the device platform. The 3D-printed platform, in combination with SMD LED and smartphone, successfully emulated a spectrometer-based instrumentation setup for curcumin detection. In short, the developed platform utilizes nitrogen-doped carbon dots with exceptionally high PLQY of 72.9 %, delivering an outstanding sensory response to curcumin, with a detection limit of 15.91 nM. The high PLQY of the nitrogen-doped carbon dots plays a significant role in achieving ultra-low detection limit. Further, considerable overlap between the absorption spectrum of curcumin and the PL spectra of NCDs enhanced the sensory response of NCDs towards curcumin. Additionally, the optimization of the reaction conditions such as pH and reaction time further improved the sensor’s response, enabling a detection limit of 15.91 nM. Further, the system was integrated with a 3D-printed platform and smartphone-based image analysis system, employing NCDs as the probe, achieving an LoD of 132.28 nM, representing highest sensory response among smartphone-based systems for real-time Cur detection in resource-limited settings. This combination of high sensitivity, selectivity, and a compact sensing platform marks a significant advancement in the field of food safety monitoring.

In summary, we have developed highly fluorescent, label free nitrogen-doped carbon dots for the efficient detection of Cur. The NCDs were synthesized through a one-step pyrolysis technique using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The NCDs exhibited blue fluorescence with emission around 449 nm and an excellent photoluminescence quantum yield of 72.9 % in water. They exhibited robust photostability under various environmental conditions and exceptional biocompatibility, making them effective fluorescent probes for sensing applications. Upon the introduction of Cur to the NCD system, significant fluorescence quenching was observed, with detailed investigations revealing the Inner Filter Effect as the primary mechanism behind the sensory response, yielding a Limit of Detection (LoD) of 15.91 nM within a linear range spanning from 0.081 µM to 51.45 µM. Moreover, the NCDs were successfully tested for Cur detection in real food matrices, such as ginger powder, turmeric powder, and curry powder, displaying excellent sensitivity and recovery rates. In addition, a 3D-printed device platform integrating smartphone-based fluorescence image analysis system was developed for curcumin detection. The device, with UV SMD LED as excitation source and quartz cuvette for sampling along with a smartphone application for analysis proved to be an effective solution for on-field detection of Cur. The platform achieved a detection limit of 132.28 nM for Cur concentration ranging from 0.13–54.00 µM. This device holds significant promise for on-site Cur detection in various food matrices, offering substantial potential at developing strategies aimed at enhancing food safety protocols.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.
